name: Thriller
description: Suspense-driven stories focused on danger, uncertainty, and escalating tension, often involving pursuit, conspiracies, or time pressure.

nodes.label: Thriller Promise
nodes.definition: The audience is promised sustained suspense — a story where danger is present, information is incomplete, tension escalates, and the outcome remains uncertain until late in the narrative.
exit_conditions: Core constraints are established that fulfill this promise
failure_modes: Claims suspense but reveals too much too early
Danger is announced but never felt
signals_in_text: Opening creates unease or establishes a threat
Reader/viewer feels the need to keep watching

nodes.label: Suspense Must Be Sustained
nodes.definition: The story must maintain a continuous sense of unresolved danger or uncertainty — the audience must consistently feel that something bad could happen.
exit_conditions: Tension is present in the majority of scenes, with brief relief used strategically
failure_modes: Long stretches without tension
Danger resolves and re-starts without connection
signals_in_text: Scene endings create questions or threats
Characters are aware of danger even in quiet moments

nodes.label: Information Is Incomplete or Unreliable
nodes.definition: The protagonist (and often the audience) must operate with incomplete, misleading, or contested information — uncertainty about who to trust, what is real, or what will happen next.
exit_conditions: At least one major information gap drives the protagonist's decisions
failure_modes: All information is available — no mystery or uncertainty
Information gaps are trivial
signals_in_text: Protagonist asks questions that aren't answered
Multiple possible explanations for events
Trust is tested repeatedly

nodes.label: Danger Must Escalate
nodes.definition: The threat must grow more serious, more personal, or more immediate as the story progresses — each revelation or event must narrow the protagonist's safety margin.
exit_conditions: Each act raises the danger level beyond the previous
typical_variants: Scope expands (conspiracy is bigger than thought)
failure_modes: Danger plateaus — same level of threat throughout
New threats are unconnected to the central danger
signals_in_text: Each setback is worse than the last
Protagonist's options narrow visibly
Body count or stakes increase

nodes.label: Protagonist Is Vulnerable
nodes.definition: The protagonist must be genuinely at risk — outmatched, isolated, or operating with significant disadvantages that make the danger feel real.
exit_conditions: Audience believes the protagonist could fail or be harmed
failure_modes: Protagonist is too powerful — danger never feels real
Safety nets are always available
signals_in_text: Protagonist struggles and fails at points
Resources are limited
Help is unreliable or unavailable

nodes.label: Psychological Thriller
nodes.definition: Thriller where the primary danger is mental or perceptual — manipulation, gaslighting, obsession, or unreliable reality — rather than physical violence.
exit_conditions: Protagonist's sanity, perception, or identity is under threat
failure_modes: Psychological manipulation is too obvious
Threat shifts to purely physical, abandoning the mental dimension
signals_in_text: Protagonist doubts their own perception
Reality is contested between characters
Mental state deteriorates under pressure

nodes.label: Conspiracy Thriller
nodes.definition: Thriller where the protagonist uncovers a hidden plot by powerful actors — each revelation expands the scope of the conspiracy and increases personal danger.
exit_conditions: Conspiracy is progressively exposed through investigation and survival
failure_modes: Conspiracy is revealed all at once rather than layered
Conspirators are incompetent
signals_in_text: Small clues lead to larger revelations
Trusted figures turn out to be compromised
Protagonist becomes a target as they learn more

nodes.label: Cat-and-Mouse Thriller
nodes.definition: Thriller structured around a pursuit dynamic between protagonist and antagonist — each trying to outmaneuver the other in an escalating game of strategy.
entry_conditions: Protagonist and antagonist are in direct opposition
exit_conditions: Both parties adapt their strategies as the game escalates
failure_modes: One side is passive — no real game
Strategies don't evolve
signals_in_text: Move and countermove structure
Both sides demonstrate intelligence
Lead changes hands

nodes.label: Ticking-Clock Thriller
nodes.definition: Thriller where an explicit deadline creates escalating urgency — a bomb, a kidnapping deadline, a countdown to catastrophe — compressing decision-making.
exit_conditions: Deadline drives pacing and forces increasingly desperate decisions
failure_modes: Deadline is too distant to create urgency
Clock is extended whenever convenient
signals_in_text: Time references in dialogue and narration
Pacing accelerates as deadline approaches
Characters make riskier choices as time runs out

nodes.label: Information Is Controlled and Contested
nodes.definition: The story's world must be one where information is power — who knows what determines who lives, who dies, and who wins. Access to truth is restricted, monitored, or manipulated.
failure_modes: Information is freely available to everyone
Characters ignore available information
signals_in_text: Characters actively seek, hide, or destroy information
Scenes involve decoding, surveillance, or interrogation

nodes.label: Protagonist Is Isolated from Help
nodes.definition: The story's setting or circumstances must isolate the protagonist — physically, socially, or institutionally — so that they cannot easily call for help or escape.
exit_conditions: Protagonist must solve the problem largely alone or with unreliable allies
typical_variants: Communication isolation (phones don't work, internet is monitored)
failure_modes: Help is always one phone call away
Protagonist chooses isolation for no good reason
signals_in_text: Attempts to get help fail or backfire
Allies are unavailable, compromised, or disbelieved

nodes.label: Trust Is Unreliable
nodes.definition: The story's social environment must make trust dangerous — allies may be compromised, institutions may be corrupt, and apparent safety may be a trap.
exit_conditions: At least one trusted figure or institution proves unreliable
typical_variants: Seemingly helpful stranger with hidden agenda
failure_modes: All allies are perfectly reliable
Betrayals are telegraphed too obviously
signals_in_text: Protagonist hesitates before sharing information
Reveals that trusted figures are compromised
Audience questions character motivations

nodes.label: Inciting Threat Scene
nodes.definition: An early scene that establishes the specific danger — the audience and/or protagonist become aware that something threatening is happening or about to happen.
exit_conditions: Audience understands that the protagonist is in danger
typical_variants: Discovery of a body or crime
Witnessing something that shouldn't have been seen
Realization that something is deeply wrong
failure_modes: Threat is too vague to create tension
Threat is so extreme it feels cartoonish
signals_in_text: Tone shifts from normalcy to unease
Protagonist's routine is disrupted by danger

nodes.label: Major Reversal / Trust Betrayal Scene
nodes.definition: A mid-story scene where a major assumption is overturned — an ally is revealed as an enemy, the problem is larger than believed, or the protagonist's understanding is fundamentally wrong.
entry_conditions: Protagonist has been operating on initial assumptions
exit_conditions: Protagonist must completely reassess the situation
failure_modes: Reversal is predictable — audience saw it coming from the start
Reversal is random rather than built through prior clues
signals_in_text: Protagonist's reaction shows genuine shock and recalculation
Prior scenes are recontextualized by the revelation

nodes.label: Climactic Confrontation Under Maximum Pressure
nodes.definition: The final confrontation where the protagonist faces the primary threat with maximum stakes, minimal resources, and no remaining safety net.
entry_conditions: All escalation has led to this point
exit_conditions: Central threat is confronted and resolved
typical_variants: Final gambit with everything at stake
failure_modes: Climax is resolved by outside intervention
Protagonist wins easily despite prior vulnerability
signals_in_text: Highest tension in the story
Protagonist uses hard-won knowledge from earlier
Resolution feels earned through accumulated struggle

nodes.label: Atmosphere of Dread and Uncertainty
nodes.definition: Thriller requires a pervasive sense that danger could strike at any moment — even quiet scenes carry an undercurrent of threat.
exit_conditions: Tone maintains tension through scene construction, pacing, and information control
failure_modes: Significant stretches feel safe and comfortable
Tension is present only in action scenes
signals_in_text: Environmental details suggest threat
Characters are watchful and on edge
Silence and stillness feel dangerous

nodes.label: False Scares and Cheap Tension
nodes.definition: Thriller fails when tension is manufactured through fake-outs, jump scares, or false alarms rather than through genuine narrative danger.
exit_conditions: Tension arises from real danger rooted in plot and character, not artificial surprises
typical_variants: Dream sequences disguised as real events
Repeated false alarms that desensitize the audience
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode — its presence indicates a genre violation
signals_in_text: Audience feels manipulated rather than genuinely scared
Tension resets to zero after each fake-out

edges.label: Promise requires sustained suspense
edges.effects_on_stakes: Tension must be maintained throughout, not just in set pieces
anti_patterns: Suspense only in isolated scenes with comfortable stretches between

edges.label: Promise requires incomplete information
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist must make decisions without full knowledge
common_alternatives: Audience may know more than protagonist (dramatic irony) or less (shared mystery)
anti_patterns: All information available from the start

edges.label: Promise requires escalating danger
edges.effects_on_stakes: Each act must be more dangerous than the last
common_alternatives: Escalation may be in scope, urgency, or personal stakes

edges.label: Promise requires vulnerable protagonist
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist must struggle and can fail
common_alternatives: Vulnerability may be physical, social, or psychological

edges.label: Promise requires dread atmosphere
edges.effects_on_character: Characters exist in a world that feels unsafe

edges.label: Information asymmetry as mental manipulation
preconditions: Information gap is used to manipulate protagonist's perception
edges.effects_on_stakes: Protagonist's sanity and perception are at stake
anti_patterns: Manipulation is too obvious to be threatening

edges.label: Information asymmetry as hidden conspiracy
preconditions: Information gap conceals an organized plot
edges.effects_on_stakes: Each revelation expands the scope of danger
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist discovers how deep the conspiracy goes

edges.label: Escalating danger as strategic pursuit
preconditions: Danger escalates through adversarial game between two parties
edges.effects_on_stakes: Each move raises the stakes for both sides
edges.effects_on_character: Both protagonist and antagonist must adapt and escalate
anti_patterns: One side is passive — no real game

edges.label: Escalating danger compressed by deadline
preconditions: Danger escalates because time is running out
edges.effects_on_stakes: Each passing moment reduces options and increases urgency

edges.label: Psychological thriller requires unreliable trust
edges.effects_on_stakes: Cannot trust own perception or others' accounts
anti_patterns: Trusted figures are all genuinely trustworthy

edges.label: Conspiracy requires information control
edges.effects_on_stakes: Conspirators control what protagonist can learn
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist must find alternative information sources

edges.label: Pursuit isolates the protagonist
edges.effects_on_stakes: Protagonist is cut off from support during the game
anti_patterns: Protagonist has a full support team throughout

edges.label: Countdown requires controlled information
edges.effects_on_stakes: Information needed to stop the clock is restricted
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist must find critical information under time pressure
anti_patterns: All needed information is immediately available

edges.label: Controlled information creates inciting threat
edges.effects_on_stakes: Protagonist discovers enough to know they're in danger but not enough to resolve it
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist is drawn into the danger by partial knowledge
anti_patterns: Threat is fully explained from the start

edges.label: Unreliable trust creates major reversal
preconditions: Protagonist has placed trust in someone or something
edges.effects_on_stakes: Betrayal escalates danger and isolates protagonist further
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist must recalculate everything they believed
anti_patterns: All initial assumptions prove correct — no reversal

edges.label: Isolation forces solo climactic confrontation
preconditions: Protagonist is isolated and must face the threat alone
edges.effects_on_stakes: No backup — protagonist faces maximum danger alone
edges.effects_on_character: Character must draw on everything they've learned and survived
common_alternatives: One unreliable ally may be present
Protagonist may engineer their own advantage
anti_patterns: Cavalry arrives to save the day

edges.label: Reversal raises stakes to climactic level
edges.effects_on_stakes: Protagonist now faces the true threat with full knowledge
edges.effects_on_character: Armed with painful truth, protagonist makes final stand
anti_patterns: Reversal has no impact on the climactic confrontation

edges.label: Fake tension violates the thriller contract
preconditions: Tension is manufactured rather than narrative-based
edges.effects_on_stakes: Audience stops trusting the story's threats
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's danger becomes a boy-who-cried-wolf situation
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern — it describes what to avoid
